content.text: is Python's low-level interface to the underlying operating system. It gives access to environment variables, process information, file system operations (create/delete/rename files and directories), and path manipulation through its sub-module
. It predates modern Python and uses a string-based, functional style.
was introduced in Python 3.4 and provides an object-oriented way to handle filesystem paths. Instead of manipulating strings and calling standalone functions, you work with
objects that carry both the path and all the operations you'd ever want to do on it. It is the modern, recommended approach for most path-related work.
Both modules ultimately talk to the same underlying operating system —
is largely a cleaner abstraction over what
Why they matter in Generative AI work
AI and data pipelines almost always need to:
Save model checkpoints and outputs to disk
Read configuration from environment variables (API keys, model paths, data directories)
Scan large folder trees recursively for files of a specific type (e.g., all
Build reproducible paths that work on macOS, Linux, and Windows
are essential for all of this — and using them incorrectly leads to brittle code that only works on the developer's machine.
in AI/ML work is reading configuration from environment variables — especially API keys, which should never be hardcoded.

import os

# Read a variable (returns None if not set)
api_key = os.environ.get("OPENAI_API_KEY")

# Read with a default fallback
model_name = os.environ.get("MODEL_NAME", "gpt-4o")

# Set a variable for the current process
os.environ["MY_VAR"] = "hello"

# Check if a variable exists
if "DATABASE_URL" in os.environ:
    print("DB configured")
if the variable doesn't exist, while

import os

# Where is the script running from?
cwd = os.getcwd()
print(cwd)  # e.g., /home/user/project

# Change directory
os.chdir("/tmp")

import os

# Create a single directory
os.mkdir("outputs")

# Create a full nested path in one call (like mkdir -p)
os.makedirs("data/raw/2024", exist_ok=True)

# Remove an empty directory
os.rmdir("outputs")

# Remove a file
os.remove("old_file.txt")

# Rename or move a file
os.rename("old_name.txt", "new_name.txt")
is important: without it, the call raises an error if the directory already exists. In scripts that run repeatedly (like training loops), always use

import os

# List files and folders in a directory
entries = os.listdir("data")
print(entries)  # ['file1.txt', 'subdir', 'file2.csv']

# Walk an entire directory tree recursively
for root, dirs, files in os.walk("data"):
    for filename in files:
        full_path = os.path.join(root, filename)
        print(full_path)
is a generator that yields a tuple of
for every directory in the tree. It is the standard way to recursively process all files in a folder.

import os

path = "/home/user/data/train.csv"

os.path.basename(path)      # "train.csv"
os.path.dirname(path)       # "/home/user/data"
os.path.splitext(path)      # ("/home/user/data/train", ".csv")
os.path.join("data", "raw", "file.txt")  # "data/raw/file.txt"
os.path.exists(path)        # True or False
os.path.isfile(path)        # True if it's a file
os.path.isdir(path)         # True if it's a directory
os.path.abspath("file.txt") # full absolute path
is critically important for cross-platform compatibility. Hard-coding
always uses the correct separator for the current OS.

from pathlib import Path

# Create a path object (does not need to exist yet)
p = Path("/home/user/data")
p = Path("relative/path")

# Current working directory
p = Path.cwd()

# Home directory
p = Path.home()

object is not a string — it is an object that knows it represents a path. It prints like a string and can be passed to most functions that expect a string path, but it also carries useful methods and properties.
The single most ergonomic feature of

from pathlib import Path

base = Path("data")
full = base / "raw" / "2024" / "train.csv"
print(full)  # data/raw/2024/train.csv
operator automatically uses the correct separator for the operating system.

from pathlib import Path

p = Path("/home/user/data/train.csv")

p.name        # "train.csv"
p.stem        # "train"
p.suffix      # ".csv"
p.parent      # Path("/home/user/data")
p.parts       # ("/", "home", "user", "data", "train.csv")

from pathlib import Path

p = Path("data/train.csv")

p.exists()   # True or False
p.is_file()  # True if it's a regular file
p.is_dir()   # True if it's a directory

from pathlib import Path

output_dir = Path("outputs/checkpoints")

# Create the full path (like mkdir -p)
output_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
suppresses the error if the directory already exists. In practice, you will use both together almost every time.
allows reading and writing file contents directly from the
object — no need to open file handles manually for simple cases:

from pathlib import Path

p = Path("notes.txt")

# Write text (overwrites if file exists)
p.write_text("Hello, world!\n", encoding="utf-8")

# Read text
content = p.read_text(encoding="utf-8")

# Write bytes
p.write_bytes(b"\x00\x01\x02")

# Read bytes
raw = p.read_bytes()
For larger files or streaming, use
which returns a standard file handle:

from pathlib import Path

data_dir = Path("data")

# List all immediate children
for item in data_dir.iterdir():
    print(item)

# Find all .txt files in the directory (non-recursive)
for txt_file in data_dir.glob("*.txt"):
    print(txt_file)

# Find all .txt files in all subdirectories (recursive)
for txt_file in data_dir.rglob("*.txt"):
    print(txt_file)
filtered for a pattern — and it is far more readable. The
pattern matches any number of subdirectories.
Most Python libraries accept both strings and
objects. When a library requires a plain string, convert with

from pathlib import Path

p = Path("data/train.csv")
path_as_string = str(p)       # "data/train.csv"
path_back = Path(path_as_string)
The diagram above shows the full picture. Your Python code sits at the top. Both
sit as an interface layer. They both communicate with the operating system's actual filesystem underneath.
internally uses many of the same calls that
provides — it is built on top of
, not a replacement of the OS itself.
Any path construction, manipulation, or inspection
Scanning directories for files matching a pattern
Lower-level OS interactions not covered by pathlib
In practice, most modern Python code uses
for environment variables, and reaches for other
Real-world example: loading a dataset directory

Here is a realistic pattern for an AI data pipeline that needs to find all text files in a data directory, read API configuration from environment variables, and save processed output to a structured folder:

import os
from pathlib import Path

# --- Configuration from environment ---
data_root = Path(os.environ.get("DATA_DIR", "data"))
output_root = Path(os.environ.get("OUTPUT_DIR", "outputs"))
model_name = os.environ.get("MODEL_NAME", "default-model")

# --- Prepare output directory ---
output_dir = output_root / model_name / "processed"
output_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

# --- Find all text files recursively ---
text_files = list(data_root.rglob("*.txt"))
print(f"Found {len(text_files)} text files")

# --- Process each file ---
for src_file in text_files:
    content = src_file.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
    
    # Preserve relative directory structure in output
    relative = src_file.relative_to(data_root)
    dest_file = output_dir / relative
    dest_file.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    
    # Write processed output (example: uppercase)
    dest_file.write_text(content.upper(), encoding="utf-8")
    print(f"Processed: {src_file} -> {dest_file}")
before writing — appears constantly in real AI pipelines.

On Windows, the default encoding is often

Resolve paths early. If your script takes a directory as an argument, convert it to an absolute resolved path immediately with
. This avoids confusion caused by relative paths when the working directory changes.
to preserve directory structure when copying or processing trees. This avoids hard-coded path slicing.
Store configurable paths as environment variables, not hardcoded strings. This makes the same script work in development, CI, and production without modification.
When writing files in loops, check and create parent directories once (before the loop) rather than inside the loop — calling
repeatedly on the same path is wasteful even though
style is consistently shorter and more expressive. The only reason to use
in new code is when targeting Python 2 (now end-of-life) or working with existing code that already uses it.
is the standard, safe way to read configuration from environment variables — essential for API keys and paths in AI applications.
turns filesystem operations into clean method calls on an object, replacing the older
objects joins paths correctly on every operating system — prefer it over string concatenation.
is the cleanest way to find all files matching a pattern across a directory tree.
before writing to a new path, and always specify
is still essential for environment variables, process-level operations, and file removal —
Modern Python AI and data pipelines use
for configuration — together they cover the vast majority of filesystem needs.
